samples.label: human
samples.text:
Initially championed by small island states (AOSIS), the push to include the 1.5°C target more prominently in the Paris agreement achieved surprising progress. While the 2°C global temperature limitation goal was adopted with difficulty at Copenhagen in 2009, demanding increased ambition seemed unrealistic to many. This issue represents a scientifically and politically characteristic node within the climate regime.

Numerous studies demonstrate that the 2°C target itself is becoming unreachable, as it would require extremely profound economic and social transformations starting today. However, national commitments submitted for COP — the "nationally determined contributions" or NDCs — revealed this radical trajectory was not being followed. Even if these commitments were genuinely implemen…

linguisticFactors.label: Lexical Diversity (TTR)
linguisticFactors.explanation: The Type-Token Ratio is 69.0% (189 unique words out of 274 total). A TTR between 60% and 85% is typical of natural, varied writing. This text falls within the expected range, suggesting healthy vocabulary variety.

linguisticFactors.label: Sentence Length Variation (CV)
linguisticFactors.explanation: The Coefficient of Variation for sentence length is 46.6% across 14 sentences (mean: 18.6 words per sentence). A CV of 45% or above is typical of natural human writing. This text shows healthy variation in sentence length.

linguisticFactors.label: Passive Voice Rate
linguisticFactors.explanation: 4 of 14 sentences (28.6%) contain passive voice constructions. Passive voice usage is at a rate typical of human writing.

linguisticFactors.label: Transition Word Rate
linguisticFactors.explanation: 0 of 14 sentences (0.0%) contain a formal transition word or phrase at the start or after a comma. A rate at or below 10% is considered unremarkable in natural prose. Transition word usage appears natural.

linguisticFactors.label: Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level
linguisticFactors.explanation: The Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level is 16.7, which corresponds roughly to a US school grade 16.7 reading level. Grades below 12 are typical for general-audience writing. Highly elevated reading levels are associated with AI text that aims to sound sophisticated.

linguisticFactors.label: Punctuation Pattern
linguisticFactors.explanation: Found 0 semicolons (0.00 per sentence) and 2 em-dashes (0.14 per sentence) across 14 sentences. Alert thresholds are 0.30 semicolons and 0.40 em-dashes per sentence. Punctuation usage appears natural.

linguisticFactors.label: Rare Word Rate
linguisticFactors.explanation: 65 of 219 qualifying words (29.7%) were not found in the common-word list. A rate between 3% and 8% is typical of natural writing. A very high proportion of uncommon words may indicate artificially elevated vocabulary, a trait sometimes seen in AI-generated text.

linguisticFactors.label: Zipf's Law Fit (R²)
linguisticFactors.explanation: The R² goodness-of-fit against Zipf's Law is -1.00, calculated across 189 unique word types from a total of 274 words. Natural text typically achieves R² above 0.92. A poor fit to Zipf's Law can indicate AI-generated text, where token sampling may produce an atypical frequency distribution.

linguisticFactors.label: Hapax Legomenon Rate
linguisticFactors.explanation: 154 of 187 unique words (82.4%) appear exactly once across 261 total words. A high hapax rate indicates diverse, natural vocabulary — a strong human writing indicator (Arxiv 2410.12341).

linguisticFactors.label: Paragraph Length Variance
linguisticFactors.explanation: Paragraph length CV is 15.5% across 4 paragraphs (mean: 65 words, SD: 10 words). Unusually uniform paragraph lengths are a pattern commonly seen in AI-generated text.

linguisticFactors.label: Function Word Density
linguisticFactors.explanation: 92 of 261 words (35.2%) are function words (articles, prepositions, pronouns, auxiliaries, conjunctions). A low function word density suggests content-word-heavy writing, a pattern associated with AI-generated text.

linguisticFactors.label: Shannon Entropy
linguisticFactors.explanation: Shannon entropy is 7.23 bits/word across 261 words (187 unique). High entropy indicates diverse, unpredictable word choices — characteristic of human writing (Venkatraman et al., 2024).

linguisticFactors.label: N-gram Repetition Rate
linguisticFactors.explanation: 1 of 258 unique trigrams (0.4%) appear more than once across 259 total trigrams. Very few repeated phrases — characteristic of varied human writing.

linguisticFactors.label: Sentence Opener Diversity
linguisticFactors.explanation: 12 unique openers out of 14 sentences (86% diversity). Most repeated: "even" (2×), "the" (2×). High opener diversity is characteristic of natural human writing.

linguisticFactors.label: Adjacent Sentence Delta
linguisticFactors.explanation: Median word-count difference between consecutive sentences is 9 words across 14 sentences. High adjacent delta indicates natural rhythm variation — a human writing signal (Desaire et al., 2023).

linguisticFactors.label: Sentence Extremes Ratio
linguisticFactors.explanation: 4 of 14 sentences (29%) are at length extremes (3 short < 11 words, 1 long > 34 words). The proportion of extreme-length sentences is within the normal range.

linguisticFactors.label: Question Frequency
linguisticFactors.explanation: 2 of 14 sentences (14%) are questions. Frequent rhetorical questions are characteristic of natural human writing (Desaire et al., 2023).

samples.topic: The road to implementation — challenges ahead
samples.text: While the diplomatic triumph of securing the Paris Agreement at COP21 in 2015 was widely celebrated, the subsequent road to implementing its provisions has illuminated the colossal and multifaceted challenges of transitioning the global economy. The fundamental hurdle facing the international community is the stark reality that the aggregate of the initial Nationally Determined Contributions submitted in Paris falls drastically short of what is scientifically required to limit global warming to 1.5 or even 2 degrees Celsius. Closing this "emissions gap" requires an unprecedented acceleration of decarbonization efforts across all sectors of the global economy, including energy generation, transportation, agriculture, and heavy industry. This necessitates not only massive technological innov…

samples.label: ai
samples.topic: The US position and Obama's role
samples.text: The participation and posture of the United States at the COP21 summit in Paris were fundamentally different from its approach in previous international climate negotiations, largely due to the strategic vision and executive actions of President Barack Obama. Historically, the United States had been a reluctant participant in global climate treaties, most notably refusing to ratify the Kyoto Protocol due to concerns over domestic economic impacts and the exemption of major developing economies like China. However, President Obama entered his second term treating climate change as a legacy-defining issue and a paramount national security threat. Understanding the limitations of a divided US Congress, which was hostile to binding international climate treaties, the Obama administration maste…

linguisticFactors.label: Sentence Opener Diversity
linguisticFactors.explanation: 12 unique openers out of 14 sentences (86% diversity). Most repeated: "the" (3×). High opener diversity is characteristic of natural human writing.

linguisticFactors.label: Adjacent Sentence Delta
linguisticFactors.explanation: Median word-count difference between consecutive sentences is 15 words across 14 sentences. High adjacent delta indicates natural rhythm variation — a human writing signal (Desaire et al., 2023).

linguisticFactors.label: Sentence Extremes Ratio
linguisticFactors.explanation: 7 of 14 sentences (50%) are at length extremes (0 short < 11 words, 7 long > 34 words). A high proportion of extreme-length sentences is characteristic of natural human writing (Desaire et al., 2023).

linguisticFactors.label: Question Frequency
linguisticFactors.explanation: 0 of 14 sentences (0%) are questions. Very few or no questions — AI text in formal register rarely uses rhetorical questions (Desaire et al., 2023).

samples.label: ai
samples.topic: Finding confidence through an unexpected extracurricular activity
samples.text:
For most of my life, I was the kind of person other people described using the word "quiet." Not shy, exactly — shyness implies a desire to connect that is thwarted by fear. I was something more settled than that. I had accepted, with the calm pragmatism of someone who has been picked last for enough teams, that I was not someone who took up space.

So when my friend Marcus told me he had signed me up for the school radio station, I assumed he was joking. Marcus had been doing the morning announcements since sophomore year, his voice booming through the hallways at seven-forty-five with a confidence I found both admirable and baffling.

"We need someone for the Thursday afternoon slot," he said. "Just an hour. Play some music, talk a little."

"I don't talk," I said. "That's my whole thing…

linguisticFactors.label: Lexical Diversity (TTR)
linguisticFactors.explanation: The Type-Token Ratio is 48.9% (311 unique words out of 636 total). A TTR between 60% and 85% is typical of natural, varied writing. This lower-than-expected ratio may indicate repetitive or formulaic language, a trait sometimes associated with AI-generated text.

linguisticFactors.label: Sentence Length Variation (CV)
linguisticFactors.explanation: The Coefficient of Variation for sentence length is 61.5% across 33 sentences (mean: 19.2 words per sentence). A CV of 45% or above is typical of natural human writing. This text shows healthy variation in sentence length.

linguisticFactors.label: Passive Voice Rate
linguisticFactors.explanation: 8 of 33 sentences (24.2%) contain passive voice constructions. Passive voice usage is at a rate typical of human writing.

linguisticFactors.label: Transition Word Rate
linguisticFactors.explanation: 0 of 33 sentences (0.0%) contain a formal transition word or phrase at the start or after a comma. A rate at or below 10% is considered unremarkable in natural prose. Transition word usage appears natural.

linguisticFactors.label: Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level
linguisticFactors.explanation: The Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level is 9.8, which corresponds roughly to a US school grade 9.8 reading level. Grades below 12 are typical for general-audience writing. This text is within an accessible, natural reading level.

linguisticFactors.label: Punctuation Pattern
linguisticFactors.explanation: Found 0 semicolons (0.00 per sentence) and 3 em-dashes (0.09 per sentence) across 33 sentences. Alert thresholds are 0.30 semicolons and 0.40 em-dashes per sentence. Punctuation usage appears natural.

linguisticFactors.label: Rare Word Rate
linguisticFactors.explanation: 79 of 491 qualifying words (16.1%) were not found in the common-word list. A rate between 3% and 8% is typical of natural writing. A very high proportion of uncommon words may indicate artificially elevated vocabulary, a trait sometimes seen in AI-generated text.

linguisticFactors.label: Zipf's Law Fit (R²)
linguisticFactors.explanation: The R² goodness-of-fit against Zipf's Law is -0.63, calculated across 311 unique word types from a total of 636 words. Natural text typically achieves R² above 0.92. A poor fit to Zipf's Law can indicate AI-generated text, where token sampling may produce an atypical frequency distribution.

linguisticFactors.label: Hapax Legomenon Rate
linguisticFactors.explanation: 211 of 309 unique words (68.3%) appear exactly once across 634 total words. A high hapax rate indicates diverse, natural vocabulary — a strong human writing indicator (Arxiv 2410.12341).

linguisticFactors.label: Paragraph Length Variance
linguisticFactors.explanation: Paragraph length CV is 49.5% across 15 paragraphs (mean: 42 words, SD: 21 words). Paragraph length variation is within the normal range.

linguisticFactors.label: Function Word Density
linguisticFactors.explanation: 324 of 634 words (51.1%) are function words (articles, prepositions, pronouns, auxiliaries, conjunctions). A high function word density is characteristic of natural human writing (Arxiv 2407.03646).

linguisticFactors.label: Shannon Entropy
linguisticFactors.explanation: Shannon entropy is 7.53 bits/word across 634 words (309 unique). High entropy indicates diverse, unpredictable word choices — characteristic of human writing (Venkatraman et al., 2024).

linguisticFactors.label: N-gram Repetition Rate
linguisticFactors.explanation: 11 of 621 unique trigrams (1.8%) appear more than once across 632 total trigrams. Very few repeated phrases — characteristic of varied human writing.

linguisticFactors.explanation: 22 unique openers out of 33 sentences (67% diversity). Most repeated: "i" (7×), "the" (2×), "but" (2×). Sentence opener diversity is within the normal range.

linguisticFactors.label: Adjacent Sentence Delta
linguisticFactors.explanation: Median word-count difference between consecutive sentences is 16 words across 33 sentences. High adjacent delta indicates natural rhythm variation — a human writing signal (Desaire et al., 2023).

linguisticFactors.explanation: 13 of 33 sentences (39%) are at length extremes (10 short < 11 words, 3 long > 34 words). A high proportion of extreme-length sentences is characteristic of natural human writing (Desaire et al., 2023).
